frameworks.name: behavioral economics / neuroeconomics
frameworks.one_line: decision-making decomposed into risk, intertemporal choice, consistency, and learning rate.

frameworks.name: intertemporal choice (delay discounting)
frameworks.one_line: trade-off between immediate and larger delayed rewards probed with real outcomes.

frameworks.name: expected-utility / risk preference
frameworks.one_line: propensity to choose lottery vs sure option indexes risk aversion.

core_question: Are decision-making processes impaired in multiple sclerosis patients, and how do these impairments relate to other cognitive domains and brain structure?
key_assumptions: behavioral-economics tasks isolate dissociable decision components (risk, patience, consistency, learning)
real-world reward outcomes elicit genuine preference signals
Brief Repeatable Battery indexes general cognitive status comparably across groups
manipulations: patient group (MS vs healthy controls; relapsing vs progressive)
decision domain (risk preference, choice consistency, delay of gratification, learning rate)
reward type (food vs money in real-life conditions)
key_findings: MS patients showed higher risk aversion than controls
trend toward steeper delay discounting (immediate over delayed rewards)
longer deliberation/reaction times, slower in progressive than relapsing patients
decision impairment correlated with verbal/visual memory, processing speed, and gray-matter volume
interpretations: MS produces significant decision-making impairment even at early disease stages
decision impairments may degrade patients' quality of life and social functioning
limitations_noted: cross-sectional design with modest control sample (n=21)
